title: Escalation of Care — Decision
oneLiner: Recognising when a ward patient is deteriorating and needs senior/ICU input to prevent cardiac arrest or death.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Unrecognised sepsis with refractory hypotension** — the patient looks “a bit off,” but the single finding that says “this is the killer” is a lactate >4 mmol/L PLUS a MAP <65 mmHg despite 30 mL/kg IV fluid bolus. Do not wait for fever or white cell count.
- **Unrecognised type 1 respiratory failure** — a patient with tachypnoea, desaturating on high-flow O₂, but the quiet chest (poor air entry) or silent pneumothorax may be overlooked. The single finding: SpO₂ <88% on FiO₂ >0.6 (60% O₂) or a rising pCO₂ on ABG with a falling pH (<7.35). This is the “can’t oxygenate, can’t ventilate” that needs immediate anaesthetic/ICU review.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** New confusion or agitation, severe dyspnoea, cyanosis, chest pain, oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr for 2 hours), mottled skin, unresponsive to initial simple interventions (e.g., fluid bolus, O₂).
- **Key exam findings:** Tachypnoea (>24/min), tachycardia (>110/min), hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg or >40 mmHg drop from baseline), hypoxia (SpO₂ <92% on air), altered consciousness (GCS <15 or drop ≥2), rising NEWS2 (≥5 or any single parameter red zone).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely** → **Less likely**, with 1-line differentiators:
  - **Sepsis** → most common cause of acute deterioration; look for source (chest, urine, line, abdomen) + lactate >2 mmol/L + qSOFA ≥2.
  - **Cardiogenic shock** → raised JVP, crackles, cool peripheries, lactate<2 initially; differentiates with echo, chest X-ray (pulmonary oedema), BNP.
  - **Pulmonary embolism** → sudden pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, DVT signs; differentiates with Wells score and CT pulmonary angiogram.
  - **Acute coronary syndrome (STEMI/NSTEMI)** → chest pain + ECG changes + troponin rise; differentiates by ECG.
  - **Haemorrhage (occult bleeding)** → orthostatic hypotension, falling Hb, abdominal distension (ruptured AAA, GI bleed); differentiates with bedside ultrasound (FAST) or CT.
  - **Anaphylaxis** → urticaria, wheeze, stridor, sudden onset after drug/exposure; responds rapidly to adrenaline.
  - **Adrenal crisis** → hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia; history of steroid use/pituitary disease.
- **Splitter:** Bedside ultrasound (e.g., focused echo, lung ultrasound, FAST) — a positive FAST or grossly dilated IVC with no collapse on inspiration suggests fluid overload (cardiogenic) rather than hypovolaemia. If lungs are dry and IVC is flat, think hypovolaemia or sepsis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):** Full A–E assessment, NEWS2 score, capillary refill, urine output (insert catheter if not monitoring), 12-lead ECG, bedside glucose, arterial blood gas (ABG) with lactate, point-of-care lactate if not on ABG, set up for eFAST/FAST if trauma or suspected abdominal bleed.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, coagulation, CRP, blood cultures, lactate, troponin, group and screen (if haemorrhage suspected), consider venous blood gas if no ABG capability.
- **Imaging:** First-line: CXR (for pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, pneumothorax). Second-line: CT head (if GCS drop), CT chest/abdomen (if suspected PE, aortic dissection, intra-abdominal sepsis), echo (if cardiogenic shock or suspected tamponade).
- **Special tests:** Urinalysis for infection, ECG (repeat if pain), VBG (if ABG not available — note it underestimates O₂ but gives pH and lactate).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** NEWS2 (National Early Warning Score) — red trigger if any single parameter is 3, or overall score ≥5; qSOFA (≥2 = 2+ of RR ≥22, SBP ≤100, altered mentation) for sepsis; NICE sepsis risk stratification (high-risk if lactate >2, hypotension, altered mentation). Use locally preferred tool.
- **Never-miss test:** **Lactate on ABG/VBG** — if not checked within 15 minutes of suspecting deterioration, you miss the most sensitive marker of tissue hypoperfusion and severe sepsis. In a confused patient, also **blood glucose** (hypoglycaemia can mimic everything).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** Start high-flow O₂ to target SpO₂ 94-98% (or 88-92% if COPD with CO₂ retention — check recent ABG). Gain IV access (2 large-bore cannulae if bleeding suspected). Give IV fluid bolus (500 mL crystalloid over 15 min, repeat if hypotensive, max 30 mL/kg for sepsis — but [check local policy] for exact volumes). If sepsis suspected, take blood cultures and start antibiotics within 1 hour (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV — but [check local policy] for first-line agent). Call senior (registrar) immediately — do not wait for all results. If NEWS2 ≥5 or any single red-zone parameter, escalate to a clinician within 30 minutes.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Continuous vital sign monitoring (minimum q15min until stable, then q1h). Repeat lactate at 2 and 4 hours to guide resuscitation. Targeted mean arterial pressure (MAP) ≥65 mmHg. In sepsis, reassess fluid responsiveness (passive leg raise, IVC ultrasound) before further fluid boluses. If hypotension persists after 30 mL/kg fluids, start vasopressors (noradrenaline) via central line — but this is ICU/ward reg decision; do not start on ward without senior input. Consider early central venous access (CVC) for vasopressor administration. Monitor urine output hourly (target >0.5 mL/kg/hr). Check for decompression of tension pneumothorax (immediate needle thoracostomy if suspect).
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Not applicable for acute escalation; patient may be transferred to ICU/ HDU, then step-down after stabilisation.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Fluids: unless contraindicated, start maintenance IV fluids (e.g., 1 litre 0.9% saline/8h, adjust for losses). VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (or 20 mg if CrCl <30 mL/min — [check local policy]). Diet/NBM status: keep NBM if there is any concern for surgical abdomen, pending CT, or if intubation likely. Observation frequency: minimum q1h for first 24h, then q4h if stable; consider continuous SpO₂ monitoring with alarms. **Document escalation plan** (e.g., ceiling of care, DNACPR status).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (for sepsis, if suspected):** Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV Q6H (first dose within 1 hour) — [check local policy] for alternatives (e.g., ceftriaxone 2 g IV once daily for community-acquired infection). If anaphylaxis: adrenaline 0.5 mg IM (1:1000, 0.5 mL). For septic shock with persistent hypotension: noradrenaline IV infusion (start 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min, titrate to MAP ≥65 mmHg) — **do not start on ward without senior instruction**.
- **Common alternatives:** Meropenem (if ESBL risk), vancomycin (if MRSA risk), co-amoxiclav (mild-moderate).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Piperacillin-tazobactam — avoid in severe penicillin allergy (use clindamycin + gentamicin as per local policy). Noradrenaline — extravasation causes tissue necrosis; always check correct IV access before starting.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment needed for first dose of piperacillin-tazobactam; subsequent doses may need frequency adjustment if CrCl <20 mL/min (e.g., Q8H — always [check local policy] or BNF).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting noradrenaline without checking the patient’s volume status** — giving vasopressor to an already hypovolaemic patient worsens perfusion and can cause ischaemic limb complications. Always ensure at least 30 mL/kg crystalloid given first (unless cardiogenic shock). **Second most dangerous: delaying antibiotics** — every hour delay in sepsis increases mortality by ~8%.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Cardiac arrest, multi-organ failure (AKI, ARDS, DIC), irreversible limb ischaemia from vasopressor extravasation, tension pneumothorax from delayed recognition, hypoglycaemia in sepsis-induced insulin resistance, metabolic acidosis.
- **Chronic:** Critical illness myopathy, post-intensive care syndrome (PTSD, cognitive impairment), chronic kidney injury, prolonged ventilation leading to ventilator-associated pneumonia, pressure sores.
sections.Prognosis: - With prompt escalation (within 1 hour of onset of red flags), mortality from septic shock is ~20-30%; each hour delay increases mortality by ~7%. For respiratory failure, early non-invasive ventilation (NIV) or intubation reduces risk of cardiac arrest. Delayed escalation is a leading cause of preventable in-hospital death.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **1. Use NEWS2 as a safety net, not a decision rule** — if your “gut feeling” says the patient is sick despite a low NEWS2, escalate anyway. This is a hallmark of good practice.
- **2. Always get an ABG in any patient with NEWS2 ≥5** — it gives lactate, pH, pO₂, pCO₂, and base deficit, all of which guide immediate management.
- **3. Document a clear escalation plan on admission** — including what parameters trigger a call (e.g., “If NEWS2 >4 or RR >24, call reg”) and the ceiling of care (e.g., ward-based only vs. ICU if appropriate). This prevents confusion during out-of-hours.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **1. “Waiting to see how they go”** — the most common error. If you are considering escalation, you should escalate. Delaying to repeat obs in 30 minutes is dangerous.
- **2. Over-reliance on single vital signs** — a normotensive patient can be in early septic shock (occult hypoperfusion with high lactate). Always assess lactate and end-organ perfusion (urine output, mental state).
- **3. Giving a fluid bolus without reassessing** — if the patient has crackles or a raised JVP after 500 mL, stop fluids and consider decompensation (e.g., cardiogenic shock). Do not blindly continue fluids.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home) —:
- **Return if:** Worsening dyspnoea, altered consciousness (confusion, drowsiness), new chest pain or palpitations, inability to pass urine, or any “worse than before” feeling.
- **Follow-up:** Either hospital review (e.g., ambulatory care) or GP within 48-72 hours to reassess for deterioration.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar) immediately if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single red-zone parameter, e.g., RR >25, SpO₂ <90% on O₂, SBP <90 mmHg)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (even if BP normal — this is occult shock)
  - GCS drop ≥2 from baseline (or new confusion)
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr for 2 consecutive hours
  - pH <7.25 on ABG (metabolic or respiratory acidosis)
  - pO₂ <8 kPa on FiO₂ >0.6 (or SpO₂ <88% despite high-flow O₂)
  - Persistent hypotension despite 30 mL/kg fluid bolus (SBP <90 mmHg or MAP <65 mmHg)
  - Suspected tension pneumothorax, tamponade, or major haemorrhage
- **Call crash team (2222) if:**
  - Cardiac or respiratory arrest
  - GCS ≤8 (or rapidly dropping)
  - Severe respiratory distress with exhaustion/cyanosis/silent chest
  - HR <40 or >140 with hypotension and unstable rhythm
  - Any patient you start CPR on
- **Independent double-check on all escalated patients:** Repeat vital signs and ABG within 15 minutes of initial call; document time of senior arrival, further plans, and a clear “ceiling of care” decision. If no response within 15 minutes, escalate to consultant.

rawMarkdown:
## Escalation of Care — Decision
**One-liner:** Recognising when a ward patient is deteriorating and needs senior/ICU input to prevent cardiac arrest or death.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Unrecognised sepsis with refractory hypotension** — the patient looks “a bit off,” but the single finding that says “this is the killer” is a lactate >4 mmol/L PLUS a MAP <65 mmHg despite 30 mL/kg IV fluid bolus. Do not wait for fever or white cell count.
- **Unrecognised type 1 respiratory failure** — a patient with tachypnoea, desaturating on high-flow O₂, but the quiet chest (poor air entry) or silent pneumothorax may be overlooked. The single finding: SpO₂ <88% on FiO₂ >0.6 (60% O₂) or a rising pCO₂ on ABG with a falling pH (<7.35). This is the “can’t oxygenate, can’t ventilate” that needs immediate anaesthetic/ICU review.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** New confusion or agitation, severe dyspnoea, cyanosis, chest pain, oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr for 2 hours), mottled skin, unresponsive to initial simple interventions (e.g., fluid bolus, O₂).
- **Key exam findings:** Tachypnoea (>24/min), tachycardia (>110/min), hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg or >40 mmHg drop from baseline), hypoxia (SpO₂ <92% on air), altered consciousness (GCS <15 or drop ≥2), rising NEWS2 (≥5 or any single parameter red zone).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely** → **Less likely**, with 1-line differentiators:
  - **Sepsis** → most common cause of acute deterioration; look for source (chest, urine, line, abdomen) + lactate >2 mmol/L + qSOFA ≥2.
  - **Cardiogenic shock** → raised JVP, crackles, cool peripheries, lactate<2 initially; differentiates with echo, chest X-ray (pulmonary oedema), BNP.
  - **Pulmonary embolism** → sudden pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, DVT signs; differentiates with Wells score and CT pulmonary angiogram.
  - **Acute coronary syndrome (STEMI/NSTEMI)** → chest pain + ECG changes + troponin rise; differentiates by ECG.
  - **Haemorrhage (occult bleeding)** → orthostatic hypotension, falling Hb, abdominal distension (ruptured AAA, GI bleed); differentiates with bedside ultrasound (FAST) or CT.
  - **Anaphylaxis** → urticaria, wheeze, stridor, sudden onset after drug/exposure; responds rapidly to adrenaline.
  - **Adrenal crisis** → hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia; history of steroid use/pituitary disease.
- **Splitter:** Bedside ultrasound (e.g., focused echo, lung ultrasound, FAST) — a positive FAST or grossly dilated IVC with no collapse on inspiration suggests fluid overload (cardiogenic) rather than hypovolaemia. If lungs are dry and IVC is flat, think hypovolaemia or sepsis.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):** Full A–E assessment, NEWS2 score, capillary refill, urine output (insert catheter if not monitoring), 12-lead ECG, bedside glucose, arterial blood gas (ABG) with lactate, point-of-care lactate if not on ABG, set up for eFAST/FAST if trauma or suspected abdominal bleed.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, coagulation, CRP, blood cultures, lactate, troponin, group and screen (if haemorrhage suspected), consider venous blood gas if no ABG capability.
- **Imaging:** First-line: CXR (for pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, pneumothorax). Second-line: CT head (if GCS drop), CT chest/abdomen (if suspected PE, aortic dissection, intra-abdominal sepsis), echo (if cardiogenic shock or suspected tamponade).
- **Special tests:** Urinalysis for infection, ECG (repeat if pain), VBG (if ABG not available — note it underestimates O₂ but gives pH and lactate).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** NEWS2 (National Early Warning Score) — red trigger if any single parameter is 3, or overall score ≥5; qSOFA (≥2 = 2+ of RR ≥22, SBP ≤100, altered mentation) for sepsis; NICE sepsis risk stratification (high-risk if lactate >2, hypotension, altered mentation). Use locally preferred tool.
- **Never-miss test:** **Lactate on ABG/VBG** — if not checked within 15 minutes of suspecting deterioration, you miss the most sensitive marker of tissue hypoperfusion and severe sepsis. In a confused patient, also **blood glucose** (hypoglycaemia can mimic everything).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** Start high-flow O₂ to target SpO₂ 94-98% (or 88-92% if COPD with CO₂ retention — check recent ABG). Gain IV access (2 large-bore cannulae if bleeding suspected). Give IV fluid bolus (500 mL crystalloid over 15 min, repeat if hypotensive, max 30 mL/kg for sepsis — but [check local policy] for exact volumes). If sepsis suspected, take blood cultures and start antibiotics within 1 hour (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV — but [check local policy] for first-line agent). Call senior (registrar) immediately — do not wait for all results. If NEWS2 ≥5 or any single red-zone parameter, escalate to a clinician within 30 minutes.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Continuous vital sign monitoring (minimum q15min until stable, then q1h). Repeat lactate at 2 and 4 hours to guide resuscitation. Targeted mean arterial pressure (MAP) ≥65 mmHg. In sepsis, reassess fluid responsiveness (passive leg raise, IVC ultrasound) before further fluid boluses. If hypotension persists after 30 mL/kg fluids, start vasopressors (noradrenaline) via central line — but this is ICU/ward reg decision; do not start on ward without senior input. Consider early central venous access (CVC) for vasopressor administration. Monitor urine output hourly (target >0.5 mL/kg/hr). Check for decompression of tension pneumothorax (immediate needle thoracostomy if suspect).
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Not applicable for acute escalation; patient may be transferred to ICU/ HDU, then step-down after stabilisation.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Fluids: unless contraindicated, start maintenance IV fluids (e.g., 1 litre 0.9% saline/8h, adjust for losses). VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (or 20 mg if CrCl <30 mL/min — [check local policy]). Diet/NBM status: keep NBM if there is any concern for surgical abdomen, pending CT, or if intubation likely. Observation frequency: minimum q1h for first 24h, then q4h if stable; consider continuous SpO₂ monitoring with alarms. **Document escalation plan** (e.g., ceiling of care, DNACPR status).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (for sepsis, if suspected):** Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV Q6H (first dose within 1 hour) — [check local policy] for alternatives (e.g., ceftriaxone 2 g IV once daily for community-acquired infection). If anaphylaxis: adrenaline 0.5 mg IM (1:1000, 0.5 mL). For septic shock with persistent hypotension: noradrenaline IV infusion (start 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min, titrate to MAP ≥65 mmHg) — **do not start on ward without senior instruction**.
- **Common alternatives:** Meropenem (if ESBL risk), vancomycin (if MRSA risk), co-amoxiclav (mild-moderate).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Piperacillin-tazobactam — avoid in severe penicillin allergy (use clindamycin + gentamicin as per local policy). Noradrenaline — extravasation causes tissue necrosis; always check correct IV access before starting.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment needed for first dose of piperacillin-tazobactam; subsequent doses may need frequency adjustment if CrCl <20 mL/min (e.g., Q8H — always [check local policy] or BNF).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting noradrenaline without checking the patient’s volume status** — giving vasopressor to an already hypovolaemic patient worsens perfusion and can cause ischaemic limb complications. Always ensure at least 30 mL/kg crystalloid given first (unless cardiogenic shock). **Second most dangerous: delaying antibiotics** — every hour delay in sepsis increases mortality by ~8%.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Cardiac arrest, multi-organ failure (AKI, ARDS, DIC), irreversible limb ischaemia from vasopressor extravasation, tension pneumothorax from delayed recognition, hypoglycaemia in sepsis-induced insulin resistance, metabolic acidosis.
- **Chronic:** Critical illness myopathy, post-intensive care syndrome (PTSD, cognitive impairment), chronic kidney injury, prolonged ventilation leading to ventilator-associated pneumonia, pressure sores.

### Prognosis
- With prompt escalation (within 1 hour of onset of red flags), mortality from septic shock is ~20-30%; each hour delay increases mortality by ~7%. For respiratory failure, early non-invasive ventilation (NIV) or intubation reduces risk of cardiac arrest. Delayed escalation is a leading cause of preventable in-hospital death.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **1. Use NEWS2 as a safety net, not a decision rule** — if your “gut feeling” says the patient is sick despite a low NEWS2, escalate anyway. This is a hallmark of good practice.
- **2. Always get an ABG in any patient with NEWS2 ≥5** — it gives lactate, pH, pO₂, pCO₂, and base deficit, all of which guide immediate management.
- **3. Document a clear escalation plan on admission** — including what parameters trigger a call (e.g., “If NEWS2 >4 or RR >24, call reg”) and the ceiling of care (e.g., ward-based only vs. ICU if appropriate). This prevents confusion during out-of-hours.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **1. “Waiting to see how they go”** — the most common error. If you are considering escalation, you should escalate. Delaying to repeat obs in 30 minutes is dangerous.
- **2. Over-reliance on single vital signs** — a normotensive patient can be in early septic shock (occult hypoperfusion with high lactate). Always assess lactate and end-organ perfusion (urine output, mental state).
- **3. Giving a fluid bolus without reassessing** — if the patient has crackles or a raised JVP after 500 mL, stop fluids and consider decompensation (e.g., cardiogenic shock). Do not blindly continue fluids.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home) — not usually relevant for escalation (most are admitted), but if patient is discharged after a period of observation:
- **Return if:** Worsening dyspnoea, altered consciousness (confusion, drowsiness), new chest pain or palpitations, inability to pass urine, or any “worse than before” feeling.
- **Follow-up:** Either hospital review (e.g., ambulatory care) or GP within 48-72 hours to reassess for deterioration.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar) immediately if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single red-zone parameter, e.g., RR >25, SpO₂ <90% on O₂, SBP <90 mmHg)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (even if BP normal — this is occult shock)
  - GCS drop ≥2 from baseline (or new confusion)
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr for 2 consecutive hours
  - pH <7.25 on ABG (metabolic or respiratory acidosis)
  - pO₂ <8 kPa on FiO₂ >0.6 (or SpO₂ <88% despite high-flow O₂)
  - Persistent hypotension despite 30 mL/kg fluid bolus (SBP <90 mmHg or MAP <65 mmHg)
  - Suspected tension pneumothorax, tamponade, or major haemorrhage
- **Call crash team (2222) if:**
  - Cardiac or respiratory arrest
  - GCS ≤8 (or rapidly dropping)
  - Severe respiratory distress with exhaustion/cyanosis/silent chest
  - HR <40 or >140 with hypotension and unstable rhythm
  - Any patient you start CPR on
- **Independent double-check on all escalated patients:** Repeat vital signs and ABG within 15 minutes of initial call; document time of senior arrival, further plans, and a clear “ceiling of care” decision. If no response within 15 minutes, escalate to consultant.
